ctx.name: Lin
ctx.notes: Lin has delivered two major features ahead of schedule but has worked late for about three weeks. I need to understand whether this pace was a temporary push or is becoming a default. Because Lin tends to brush off concern, I should ask directly what is driving the load now that those launches are done. I also want clarity on what work only Lin should own versus what could be delegated, since sustained late nights at this level can hide a distribution or autonomy problem.

focusPoints.label: Late nights — temporary push or default pace?
focusPoints.reason: Notes say Lin worked late for about three weeks after delivering two major features ahead of schedule. Could be a short sprint to close launches, or it might be becoming the new operating rhythm — worth clarifying before assuming it will naturally fade.

focusPoints.label: What’s driving the load right now?
focusPoints.reason: Manager notes say Lin tends to brush off concern. One possibility is there’s an ongoing dependency, decision bottleneck, or un-delegated work continuing after the launches — worth asking directly what’s actually keeping the workload high now.

focusPoints.label: Work only Lin should own vs delegate
focusPoints.reason: Whether a Head of Product is delegating effectively is a common failure mode when senior leaders keep late hours — the conversation should separate truly non-delegable responsibilities from work that could be owned by others to restore sustainable pace.

focusPoints.label: What Lin is saying yes to now
focusPoints.reason: Bi-weekly check-ins are where senior leaders can quietly drift from the few bets that matter into ongoing firefighting. Whether the current focus is the right mix of priorities vs carryover from the last launches is worth re-checking.

transcript.question: What's been slower or harder than it should have been?
transcript.answer: The last stretch shipped, but it was heavier than I made it sound.

transcript.question: When you say it was 'heavier' — what was making it heavy?
transcript.answer: Late nights came from carrying decision bottlenecks and final reviews.

transcript.question: Decision bottlenecks — where are they stacking up most right now?
transcript.answer: Some of that work could have moved to the team sooner.

transcript.question: How's the last two weeks actually felt — energy-wise?
transcript.answer: I am proud of the outcomes, but the pace should not be normal.

transcript.question: proud outcomes, pace — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
transcript.answer: I still do not get enough substantive feedback on key decisions.

transcript.question: still enough substantive — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
transcript.answer: I want clearer ownership boundaries so I am not default escalation.

transcript.question: What are you actually focused on this week, and why those things?
transcript.answer: Next I want to hand off one stream end-to-end and stay out of the middle.

transcript.question: What would make the next two weeks feel more sustainable?
transcript.answer: If we reset scope now, the next month is sustainable.

transcript.question: What work are you still holding that someone else could own?
transcript.answer: I want to focus first on unblocking the one dependency that is slowing the team the most this week.
